claims.claim: Seven Malaysian tankers including Petronas and MISC vessels were stranded in the Strait of Hormuz after Iran blocked the waterway on 28 February 2026.
claims.source: BERNAMA, 28 March 2026, https://web11.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2538464; IMO, 24 April 2026, https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/no-safe-transit-through-hormuz-imo-secretary-general.aspx
claims.correction: BERNAMA verified seven Malaysian-company tankers were awaiting clearance by 28 March 2026 and were not detained. IMO separately verified severe Hormuz disruption after the conflict. The claim compresses the timeline by implying the seven Malaysian tankers were stranded immediately on 28 February.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; later official reports on 7 and 17 April clarified staged passage and one damaged vessel.

claims.claim: PM Anwar secured passage through a direct call to Iran's president.
claims.source: BERNAMA, 7 April 2026, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2542200; PMO speech PDF, 6 April 2026, https://www.pmo.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/060426-KEYNOTE-ADDRESS-BY-YAB-PM-ON-KUALA-LUMPUR-ANKARA-DIALOGUE-2026.pdf
claims.correction: Official Malaysian reporting says high-level engagement between Anwar and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian on 26 March enabled clearance for seven vessels. It should be framed as securing Iranian permission/clearance, not guaranteeing immediate physical transit.
claims.stale_check: Later reports show permission did not mean all ships had already passed.

claims.claim: Malaysia was not on Iran's original safe-passage list.
claims.source: Iran-related statement reported by Iranian Consulate/Foreign Minister and cited in Economic Times, 26 March 2026, https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/industry/energy/oil-gas/hormuz-route-opened-for-india-says-iran-fm-araghchi/articleshow/129814058.cms; Reuters/Straits Times on Iran note to UN/IMO, 25 March 2026, https://www.straitstimes.com/world/middle-east/iran-says-non-hostile-ships-can-transit-strait-of-hormuz-ft-reports
claims.correction: The verified list named China, Russia, India, Iraq and Pakistan as friendly nations. Malaysia was not in that named list, but Iran also used a broader 'non-hostile vessels' category, so 'original safe-passage list' should be specified as the named friendly-country list.
claims.stale_check: Malaysia subsequently obtained clearance through direct engagement.

claims.claim: Three Petronas, two MISC, one Sapura, one Sepadu vessel were awaiting clearance.
claims.source: New Straits Times citing Foreign Minister Mohamad Hasan, 28 March 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/03/1405226/seven-malaysian-tankers-awaiting-safe-window-transit-strait-hormuz; BERNAMA, 28 March 2026, https://web11.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2538464
claims.correction: BERNAMA verifies Petronas and Sapura but not all company counts in the accessible text; NST reports the exact breakdown from the foreign minister. Also spell Shapadu/Sepadu consistently with the registered company name used by source.

claims.claim: Seven Malaysian tankers cleared to pass Hormuz after PM Anwar's call to Iran's president.
claims.source: BERNAMA, 17 April 2026, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2546031; BERNAMA, 7 April 2026, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php/world/news.php?id=2542053
claims.correction: Iran approved all seven, but they were to transit in stages; by 17 April only two had passed and one vessel had technical problems. 'Cleared to pass' is acceptable only if it means approval, not completed passage.
claims.stale_check: Later 15 May reports indicated a fourth Malaysia-linked vessel had transited, so the status evolved after the original clearance.

claims.claim: Malaysia is a CMF member on paper.
claims.source: Combined Maritime Forces official member list, accessed 17 May 2026, https://combinedmaritimeforces.com/about/
claims.correction: CMF lists Malaysia among its 47 member nations. 'On paper' is interpretive and should be separated from the factual membership claim.

claims.claim: Malaysia has no deployed naval assets in the Gulf.
claims.source: BERNAMA, 8 April 2026, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2542401; New Straits Times, 1 April 2026, https://www.nst.com.my/amp/news/nation/2026/04/1407862/no-military-escort-malaysian-vessels-strait-hormuz
claims.correction: Primary-accessible Malaysian official statements verify no RMN escort was required/deployed for these vessels, but I did not find a primary source proving Malaysia had no deployed naval assets anywhere in the Gulf.

claims.claim: Japan runs an SDF mission near Hormuz without joining the US coalition.
claims.source: Japan Ministry of Defense, Information Gathering Activities by the SDF in the Middle East, https://www.mod.go.jp/en/d_architecture/m_east/index.html; Japan MOD/JMSDF mission page, https://www.mod.go.jp/msdf/en/about/role/
claims.correction: Japan runs SDF information-gathering in the Middle East, but Japan's MOD explicitly says the mission area does not include the Strait of Hormuz or Persian Gulf. 'Near Hormuz' overstates proximity unless the exact operating area is given.

claims.claim: South Korea maintains a permanent Cheonghae Unit in the Gulf of Aden.
claims.source: Republic of Korea Ministry of National Defense white paper PDF noting deployment since March 2009, https://www.mnd.go.kr/user/mndEN/upload/pblictn/PBLICTNEBOOK_201705180357180050.pdf; Korea.net official ROK site, 30 March 2022, https://www.korea.net/NewsFocus/Korea_in_photos/view?articleId=212538
claims.correction: The unit is rotational rather than 'permanent' in the sense of one fixed crew; a continuing rotational deployment since 2009 is supported.

claims.claim: Malaysia contributes nothing operational.
claims.source: BERNAMA, 8 April 2026, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2542401; Combined Maritime Forces official member list, https://combinedmaritimeforces.com/about/
claims.correction: For the seven vessels, Malaysia did not provide a naval escort, but 'nothing operational' is broader than the evidence. CMF membership may include staff, liaison or other non-ship contributions not checked here.

claims.claim: Brent crude hit $126/barrel.
claims.source: The Guardian market report, 30 April 2026, https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/apr/30/oil-price-news-highest-since-2022-us-iran-ceasefire-strait-of-hormuz; EIA April STEO, https://www.eia.gov/outlooks/steo/?tableNumber=24
claims.correction: Brent hitting about $126 is supported for 30 April 2026 by market reporting; EIA's April STEO gives a March average of $103/b and Q2 forecast peak of $115/b, so the card should date the $126 figure and distinguish intraday/futures/spot price.
claims.stale_check: If the card was written before 30 April 2026, this figure may have been premature; by 30 April it became supportable.

claims.claim: The subsidy bill jumped to RM4 billion per month.
claims.source: Malaysia Ministry of Finance press citation, March 2026, https://www.mof.gov.my/portal/en/news/press-citations/subsidies-under-ron95-budi-diesel-hit-rm4-bln-monthly-mof
claims.correction: MOF said RON95 and diesel subsidies, about RM0.7b in January 2026, were estimated to rise to around RM4b per month with crude at US$100/b. It should specify fuel subsidies under RON95/BUDI Diesel, not all subsidies.
claims.stale_check: Later MOF/NST reporting suggested even higher March-April figures, so RM4b may be dated.

claims.claim: Higher crude boosts Petronas revenue.
claims.source: No primary source found within reasonable effort for this specific 2026 claim; Petronas investor results would be needed to verify revenue impact.
claims.correction: Frame as an economic expectation unless backed by Petronas financial results or official fiscal projections. Higher crude can raise upstream revenue but may be offset by imports, costs and market structure.

claims.claim: The RM4B subsidy and rising costs land on B40 households, hawkers, fishers and rural commuters.
claims.source: MOF verified RM4b subsidy estimate, https://www.mof.gov.my/portal/en/news/press-citations/subsidies-under-ron95-budi-diesel-hit-rm4-bln-monthly-mof; no primary distributional-impact source found for the named groups.
claims.correction: The RM4b figure is verified, but the specific distributional claim about B40 households, hawkers, fishers and rural commuters needs household expenditure or sectoral impact evidence.

claims.claim: Malaysia banned US warships while seeking Iran's goodwill.
claims.source: MalaysiaNow report, 19 March 2026, https://www.malaysianow.com/news/2026/03/19/putrajaya-says-wont-allow-any-more-us-warships-to-dock-as-critics-pan-anwars-double-face
claims.correction: I found secondary reporting that Putrajaya would stop accepting port-of-call applications from US warships, but no primary Malaysian government document or official statement confirming a formal ban. 'Banned' should not be used without a primary source.

claims.claim: Non-alignment preserves diplomatic flexibility.
claims.source: BERNAMA, 17 April 2026, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2546031
claims.correction: Anwar said Malaysia takes a balanced and independent stance, but the claimed causal effect, preserving flexibility, is analysis rather than a directly verifiable fact.

claims.claim: ~2,000 ships are blocked globally.
claims.source: IMO, 24 April 2026, https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/no-safe-transit-through-hormuz-imo-secretary-general.aspx; UN Geneva, 8 April 2026, https://www.ungeneva.org/en/news-media/news/2026/04/117460/iran-ceasefire-raises-hopes-reopening-key-strait-hormuz
claims.correction: IMO/UN verified around 20,000 seafarers trapped on vessels in the Persian Gulf, not clearly '~2,000 ships blocked globally' in the primary sources found. Some secondary reports cite hundreds or 1,600-2,000 vessels; the denominator and scope should be sourced precisely.

claims.claim: Traffic drops 90%.
claims.source: Straits Times citing Kpler, 5 March 2026, https://www.straitstimes.com/business/oil-tanker-traffic-through-strait-of-hormuz-down-90-amid-war-on-iran-kpler; S&P Global Commodities at Sea, 30 April 2026, https://www.spglobal.com/energy/en/news-research/latest-news/shipping/043026-strait-of-hormuz-traffic-drops-to-14-ships-with-most-leaving-persian-gulf-cas
claims.correction: The 90% figure is supported by industry data reporting, but I did not find an open primary dataset or regulator publication verifying it. It should specify 'oil tanker traffic' and date, not all traffic generically.
claims.stale_check: Traffic changed substantially over March-April; any percentage requires a date.

claims.claim: Japan and South Korea built institutional Gulf presence without abandoning independent policy.
claims.source: Japan MOD, https://www.mod.go.jp/en/d_architecture/m_east/index.html; ROK MND white paper, https://www.mnd.go.kr/user/mndEN/upload/pblictn/PBLICTNEBOOK_201705180357180050.pdf
claims.correction: South Korea's Cheonghae deployment in the Gulf of Aden is verified; Japan's Middle East SDF activity is verified but explicitly excludes the Strait of Hormuz and Persian Gulf. 'Gulf presence' is too broad for Japan unless referring to Gulf of Aden/Oman-adjacent areas rather than the Persian Gulf/Hormuz.

claims.claim: Malaysia chose neither, and the gap showed.
claims.source: No primary source directly supports this evaluative conclusion; related official facts from BERNAMA and CMF listed above.
claims.correction: This is analysis. It should be tied to verified facts: CMF membership, no Malaysian escort for the seven vessels, and reliance on diplomatic clearance.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Seven Malaysian tankers including Petronas and MISC vessels were stranded in the Strait of Hormuz after Iran blocked the waterway on 28 February 2026.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The broader Hormuz disruption began after 28 February, but the seven Malaysian tankers were reported awaiting clearance on 28 March and officials said they were not detained. The phrasing compresses a month-long timeline.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Seven Malaysian tankers cleared to pass Hormuz after PM Anwar's call to Iran's president.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Iran approved the seven, but official updates show staged passage and one damaged vessel; 'cleared to pass' may imply all had already transited.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Japan runs an SDF mission near Hormuz without joining the US coalition.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Japan's official MOD page says the SDF information-gathering mission does not include the Strait of Hormuz or the Persian Gulf.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Malaysia banned US warships while seeking Iran's goodwill.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: I found secondary reporting but no primary Malaysian government source confirming a formal ban; 'banned' is too specific without official confirmation.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: No primary source found; secondary report: https://www.malaysianow.com/news/2026/03/19/putrajaya-says-wont-allow-any-more-us-warships-to-dock-as-critics-pan-anwars-double-face

cardinal_sin_flags.why: No escort for these vessels is supported, but 'nothing operational' is broader than the evidence and not proven across CMF or other deployments.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: when ~2,000 ships are blocked globally and traffic drops 90%
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Primary IMO/UN sources verify around 20,000 seafarers trapped, but not the exact '~2,000 ships blocked globally' phrasing; 90% appears in industry-data reporting and needs date/scope qualification.

omitted_facts: BERNAMA reported on 28 March 2026 that Foreign Minister Mohamad Hasan said the seven Malaysian vessels were not detained, but waiting for a safe window: https://web11.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2538464

BERNAMA reported on 17 April 2026 that Iran had approved all seven vessels, but only two had passed and one vessel had technical problems preventing departure: https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2546031

IMO reported on 24 April 2026 that 29 vessel attacks had been verified since the beginning of the conflict, causing at least 10 seafarer deaths: https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/no-safe-transit-through-hormuz-imo-secretary-general.aspx

Malaysia's Deputy Defence Minister said on 8 April 2026 there was no need for a Royal Malaysian Navy escort after Iran cleared the vessels, tying the non-deployment to Iran's cooperation rather than only to Malaysian capability: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2542401

MOF's RM4b monthly figure applied to RON95 and diesel subsidies under BUDI95/BUDI Diesel and was an estimate at crude around US$100/b, not a blanket national cost number: https://www.mof.gov.my/portal/en/news/press-citations/subsidies-under-ron95-budi-diesel-hit-rm4-bln-monthly-mof
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only two related T4A issues, which are circular and not primary sources. It does not cite Malaysian government statements, Iranian statements, IMO/UN maritime safety updates, CMF membership records, Japan MOD documentation, ROK defence documents, MOF subsidy statements, or market-data sources. Political-spectrum coverage is effectively absent: no Malaysian government primary source, no opposition or civil-society source, no industry shipping data source, no international maritime authority, and no affected seafarer or business voice. The source base is therefore extremely narrow.
overall_assessment: The analysis captures a real broad storyline: Malaysian-linked vessels were delayed around Hormuz, Anwar's Iran engagement helped secure clearance, Malaysia is a CMF member, fuel subsidies rose sharply, and Hormuz traffic was badly disrupted. However, several cards overcompress timelines, blur approval with completed transit, overstate Japan's proximity to Hormuz, use an unverified 'US warship ban', and present unsourced operational and distributional conclusions as fact. It is usable only after tightening dates, scopes, and source-backed wording.
